questions.question: Define CPU Utilization and Throughput as scheduling criteria. How are they related?
keyPoints: CPU Utilization = percentage of time CPU is busy executing processes (typically 40-90%)
Throughput = number of processes completed per unit time (e.g., processes/hour)
Higher CPU utilization generally leads to higher throughput as CPU is not idle
They are directly related - when CPU is utilized efficiently, more work gets done

questions.question: Explain Turnaround Time and Waiting Time. How do they differ?
keyPoints: Turnaround Time = Total time from process arrival to completion (includes waiting + execution + I/O)
Waiting Time = Total time process spends in ready queue waiting for CPU
Waiting Time is a component of Turnaround Time
Turnaround Time = Waiting Time + Execution Time + I/O Time

questions.question: Why is Response Time critical for interactive systems? Explain with an example.
keyPoints: Response Time = time from request submission to first response
For interactive systems (terminals, web browsers), users expect immediate feedback
Example: In a time-sharing system, typing a command should show output within milliseconds
Long response times make systems feel sluggish and unresponsive

questions.question: What is meant by scheduling criteria trade-off? Give an example.
keyPoints: Trade-off means optimizing one criterion may degrade another
SJF minimizes average waiting time but may cause starvation for long processes
Batch systems prioritize throughput while interactive systems prioritize response time
System designers must balance competing criteria based on system type

questions.question: Define Fairness in CPU scheduling. How does it relate to starvation?
keyPoints: Fairness ensures every process gets a fair share of CPU time
No process should wait indefinitely for CPU service
Starvation occurs when fairness is violated - some processes never get CPU
Aging (gradually increasing priority) is one technique to prevent starvation

questions.question: Differentiate between CPU-bound and I/O-bound processes in terms of scheduling criteria.
keyPoints: CPU-bound processes spend most time in CPU execution (long CPU bursts)
I/O-bound processes spend most time waiting for I/O (short CPU bursts)
For CPU-bound: prioritize CPU Utilization and Throughput
For I/O-bound: prioritize Response Time and fairness

questions.question: Consider three processes P1, P2, and P3 with burst times 10, 5, and 8 respectively, all arriving at time 0. Using Shortest Job First (SJF) scheduling: (a) Draw the Gantt chart (b) Calculate waiting time for each process (c) Calculate average waiting time (d) Calculate turnaround time for each process (e) Calculate average turnaround time
keyPoints: Waiting Time: P1=13, P2=0, P3=5
Average Waiting Time = (13+0+5)/3 = 6 units
Turnaround Time: P1=23, P2=5, P3=13
Average Turnaround Time = (23+5+13)/3 = 13.67 units

questions.question: Explain all the scheduling criteria used to evaluate CPU scheduling algorithms. For each criterion, specify: (a) What it measures (b) Why it is important (c) Which type of system prioritizes it (batch/interactive/real-time)
keyPoints: CPU Utilization - measures CPU busyness, important for efficiency, batch systems
Throughput - measures completion rate, important for productivity, batch systems
Turnaround Time - measures completion speed, important for job completion, batch systems
Waiting Time - measures queue delay, important for responsiveness, all systems
Response Time - measures initial response, critical for interactivity, interactive systems
Fairness - measures equal treatment, prevents starvation, all systems

questions.question: A process arrives at time 0 with burst time 24ms. Another process arrives at time 2 with burst time 4ms. Using Round Robin with time quantum 3ms: (a) Show the execution timeline (b) Calculate waiting time for each process (c) Calculate turnaround time for each process (d) What is the average waiting time and average turnaround time?
keyPoints: P1 waiting: 3ms (at time 2-3 when P2 ran) = 1ms, then waited from 10-13=3ms, 16-19=3ms, 22-24=2ms = Total 9ms
P2 waiting: 0ms (arrived at 2, started at 3 immediately) = 0ms or actually waited 1ms (2-3) = 1ms
Average waiting time depends on exact calculation approach, turnaround P1=24, P2=10

questions.question: Compare FCFS and SJF scheduling algorithms based on all scheduling criteria. Show why SJF gives minimum average waiting time but may cause starvation.
keyPoints: FCFS: Simple, fair, but poor average waiting time; good for throughput if bursts are similar
SJF: Minimizes average waiting time and turnaround time, but may cause starvation for long processes
Fairness: FCFS is fairer, SJF may starve long processes
Response Time: SJF has lower average response time for short processes
Example with bursts: P1=10, P2=5, P3=2 shows FCFS wait times vs SJF wait times difference
